Expected misread error under 4-hour window on a 12-hour clock with identical hands

A perfect 12-hour analog clock has an hour hand and a minute hand of equal length, so they are indistinguishable.

Model a displayed configuration by an unordered pair {h,m} with 0 \leq h,m < 12, where a "valid time reading"
(h as the hour-hand position, m as the minute-hand position) must satisfy
  h = ⌊h⌋ + m/12.                                             (1)

If the hands are swapped, the alternate reading (m as the hour-hand position, h as the minute-hand position)
is valid exactly when
  m = ⌊m⌋ + h/12.                                             (2)

Call a configuration {h,m} ambiguous if it admits two distinct valid readings, i.e. (1) and (2) both hold and h \neq m.
Anna's biological rhythm guarantees that for any ambiguous configuration she sees, the two candidate times differ by at most 4 hours:
  |h - m| \leq 4.

Anna picks uniformly at random from all such ambiguous configurations satisfying |h-m|\leq4. Let E be the actual
error |h-m| for the chosen configuration. Write E as a reduced fraction a/b and output a+b.

Solution.
Step 1 (set_up_fractional_part_equations + decomposition_by_integer_parts)
Let i = ⌊h⌋ and j = ⌊m⌋, so i,j \in {0,1,...,11}. From (1) and (2) we have the linear system
  h = i + m/12,
  m = j + h/12.
Solve for h and m in terms of (i,j).

From the first equation m = 12(h - i). Substitute into the second:
  12(h - i) = j + h/12
\Rightarrow 144(h - i) = 12j + h
\Rightarrow 143h = 144i + 12j
\Rightarrow h = (144i + 12j)/143.

Then
  m = 12(h - i) = 12( (144i + 12j)/143 - i )
               = (12i + 144j)/143.

Also observe
  h = i + (12j + i)/143,
  m = j + (12i + j)/143.
The fractional add-ons are in [0,1), and equal 1 only when i=j=11 (which would give h=m=12, outside [0,12)).
Hence for every pair (i,j) \neq (11,11), these formulas indeed produce a unique (h,m) with floors i and j.

Step 2 (derive_discrete_offsets)
Compute the difference:
  h - m = (144i + 12j - 12i - 144j)/143
        = 132(i - j)/143
        = 12(i - j)/13.
Therefore every ambiguous configuration has error
  |h - m| = 12|i - j|/13.                                      (3)

In particular, the possible errors are discrete multiples of 12/13.

Step 3 (apply_error_window_and_count_configurations_by_offset)
The window condition |h-m| \leq 4 together with (3) gives
  12|i - j|/13 \leq 4
\Rightarrow |i - j| \leq 52/12
\Rightarrow |i - j| \leq 4
since i-j is an integer.

Ambiguity requires h\neqm, hence i\neqj, so
  n := |i - j| \in {1,2,3,4}.

For each fixed n \in {1,2,3,4}, count how many unordered configurations {h,m} arise with |i-j|=n.

Because each (i,j) with i>j produces exactly one configuration {h,m}, and swapping i,j produces the same unordered configuration,
the number of configurations with |i-j|=n equals the number of integer pairs (i,j) with 0\leqj<i\leq11 and i-j=n.

For i-j=n, the valid j are 0,1,...,11-n, giving exactly (12-n) choices. Hence:
- error = 12n/13 occurs in exactly (12-n) ambiguous configurations.

Total number of ambiguous configurations under the window is
  (12-1)+(12-2)+(12-3)+(12-4) = 11+10+9+8 = 38.

Step 4 (compute_expected_value)
Since Anna chooses uniformly among these 38 configurations, the expected error is
  E = (1/38) * Σ_{n=1}^4 (12-n) * (12n/13)
    = (12/(13 \cdot 38)) * Σ_{n=1}^4 n(12-n).

Compute the sum:
  Σ_{n=1}^4 n(12-n) = Σ_{n=1}^4 (12n - n^2)
                    = 12(1+2+3+4) - (1^2+2^2+3^2+4^2)
                    = 12 \cdot 10 - (1+4+9+16)
                    = 120 - 30
                    = 90.

Thus
  E = (12 \cdot 90)/(13 \cdot 38) = 1080/494 = 540/247
in lowest terms (since gcd(540,247)=1).

Therefore a=540, b=247, and the requested output is
  a+b = 540+247 = 787.

Answer: 787.

Uniqueness Test
1) (Discrete parameterization certificate)
Equations (1)-(2) force integer parts i=⌊h⌋ and j=⌊m⌋ and yield the explicit unique solution
  h=(144i+12j)/143,  m=(12i+144j)/143
for each (i,j)\neq(11,11). Hence ambiguous configurations correspond to a finite, explicitly describable set.

2) (Error values are forced)
From the solved form, the error satisfies the exact identity
  |h-m| = 12|i-j|/13,
so every possible error magnitude is uniquely determined by the integer parameter n=|i-j|.

3) (Window bounds n uniquely)
The constraint |h-m|\leq4 becomes 12n/13\leq4, forcing n\in{1,2,3,4} (excluding n=0 because ambiguity requires h\neqm).
Thus the admissible set of configurations is uniquely determined.

4) (Uniform expectation is uniquely determined)
For each n, the number of admissible unordered configurations is exactly 12-n (count of i>j with i-j=n),
so the multiset of errors is unique. Therefore the average E is a uniquely determined rational number.
Reducing E to lowest terms gives a unique pair (a,b), hence a+b is uniquely 787.

787
